module.title: Module 6: Operations, Decision-Making & Emergency Procedures
module.description: The core Operations area of the Part 107 exam (~35-45% of questions): Crew Resource Management, the remote PIC's authority and responsibility, human physiology and fitness for flight, Aeronautical Decision-Making and the five hazardous attitudes with their antidotes, risk management (PAVE / 3P), radio and traffic-pattern procedures at non-towered airports, maintenance and preflight, emergency procedures and lost-link, night operations, security, and maintaining visual line of sight.
learning_objectives: Apply Crew Resource Management (CRM) and identify the remote PIC's final authority and responsibilities under Part 107.
Recognize physiological factors that impair a remote pilot, including drugs and alcohol, and apply the 8-hour / 0.04 BAC rule.
Use Aeronautical Decision-Making (ADM), identify the five hazardous attitudes, and state each antidote verbatim.
Apply risk-management frameworks (PAVE, the 3P model, IMSAFE) to a planned operation.
Use correct radio phraseology and traffic-pattern awareness to see and avoid manned aircraft at non-towered airports.
Perform proper maintenance, recordkeeping, and preflight inspection.
Respond correctly to in-flight emergencies (lost link, flyaway, GPS loss, low battery, fire) and conduct safe night operations while maintaining VLOS.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Crew Resource Management & the Remote PIC's Authority
learning_objectives: Define Crew Resource Management and identify the resources available to a remote pilot.
State the remote PIC's final authority and responsibilities under 14 CFR 107.19.
Distinguish the roles of the remote PIC, the person manipulating the controls, and the visual observer.

content.html:
What is Crew Resource Management?

Crew Resource Management (CRM) is the effective use of all resources available to conduct a flight safely. Those resources fall into three groups: human resources (a visual observer, a second remote pilot, ground crew, bystanders helping with crowd control, ATC, a weather briefer), hardware (the aircraft, control station, batteries, lighting, checklists), and information (weather, NOTAMs, airspace data, the manufacturer's manual). Even a single-pilot drone operation benefits from CRM: you are still managing checklists, the aircraft, weather information, and the people around your launch site.

Good CRM is about communication, situational awareness, and workload management. Before flight, the remote PIC should brief everyone involved, assign clear roles, agree on how the crew will communicate (verbal callouts, radios, hand signals), and confirm what to do if something goes wrong. When a visual observer is used, the RPIC and VO must be able to communicate at all times so the VO can pass traffic and hazard information back to the pilot.

content.text: CRM is not only for big crews. A solo remote pilot still 'manages a crew' of one — plus checklists, the aircraft, and information sources. The goal is to never be overloaded to the point that safety suffers.

content.html:
The Remote PIC: final authority and responsibility

Under 14 CFR 107.19, every Part 107 operation must have one Remote Pilot in Command (RPIC). The RPIC is directly responsible for, and the final authority over, the operation. That responsibility cannot be delegated away. The RPIC must:

  • Ensure the operation poses no undue hazard to people, aircraft, or property if control is lost.
  • Ensure the small unmanned aircraft complies with all of Part 107.
  • Have the ability to direct the aircraft to ensure compliance — and to immediately take direct control if a non-certificated person is manipulating the controls.

A person who does not hold a remote pilot certificate may fly the aircraft only if a certificated RPIC is supervising and can immediately take direct control. The RPIC is still responsible for everything that happens.

rows: Final authority and responsibility for the entire operation (107.19); ensures compliance and safety.
No, if directly supervised by an RPIC who can immediately take control
Flies the aircraft under the RPIC's supervision.
Helps the RPIC maintain awareness of the aircraft's position and scan for traffic; must be able to communicate with the RPIC.

content.text: Only ONE person may act as RPIC for a given operation at a time, and a remote pilot may act as RPIC (or manipulate the controls) for only ONE aircraft at a time (107.35). You cannot fly two drones at once under Part 107.

content.alt: Diagram of a remote pilot crew showing RPIC, person manipulating controls, and visual observer with communication links
content.caption: Figure: A Part 107 crew — the RPIC holds final authority; a non-certificated person may fly only under direct supervision; the VO scans and communicates traffic.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: CRM = using all available resources (people, hardware, information) to fly safely; brief the crew and assign roles before launch.
Under 107.19 the Remote PIC is the final authority and is directly responsible for the operation.
A non-certificated person may manipulate the controls only if an RPIC supervises and can immediately take direct control.
One RPIC per operation, and one aircraft per pilot at a time (107.35); a visual observer is optional but must be able to communicate with the RPIC.

flashcards.definition: The effective use of all available resources — human (VO, crew, ATC), hardware, and information — to conduct a flight safely.
flashcards.hint_1: It's about communication and situational awareness, not just having a big crew.

flashcards.definition: The Remote Pilot in Command is directly responsible for, and is the final authority over, the operation; must ensure compliance and be able to direct/immediately control the aircraft.
An optional crew member (no certificate required) who helps the RPIC keep the aircraft in sight and scan for traffic; must be able to communicate with the RPIC.
A person may not operate or act as remote PIC for more than one unmanned aircraft at the same time.

images_needed.caption: Crew diagram showing RPIC (final authority), person manipulating controls under supervision, and a visual observer, with communication arrows between them.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Physiological Factors & Fitness for Flight
learning_objectives: Identify physiological factors that degrade pilot performance: dehydration, hyperventilation, stress, fatigue, and impaired vision.
State the alcohol and drug rules: 8 hours bottle-to-throttle, 0.04 BAC, and no operating while impaired.
Use the IMSAFE checklist to self-assess fitness before each flight.

content.html:
Your body is part of the aircraft system

A drone never gets tired or dehydrated — but the remote pilot does, and a degraded pilot makes degraded decisions. The FAA expects every remote pilot to be in a physical and mental condition to safely operate. Several common physiological factors quietly erode judgment, vision, and reaction time:

  • Dehydration & heat stress: Working outdoors in sun and heat causes fluid loss that reduces alertness, slows thinking, blurs vision, and causes headaches and fatigue — often before you notice. Drink water, take shade breaks, and watch for early signs.
  • Stress: Physical, physiological, and psychological stress narrow attention and degrade decision-making. High stress can cause you to fixate or to miss obvious hazards.
  • Fatigue: One of the most insidious hazards — fatigue degrades attention, reaction time, and judgment as much as alcohol can. Acute fatigue (one bad night) and chronic fatigue (long-term) both impair you. Don't fly tired.
  • Hyperventilation: Abnormally rapid or deep breathing — usually triggered by stress or anxiety — flushes out too much carbon dioxide, causing lightheadedness, tingling, visual impairment, and even loss of consciousness. The remedy is to slow the breathing rate (and breathe into a paper bag if needed) until normal breathing returns.

Vision and scanning

Because the legal standard is visual line of sight, the pilot's vision is mission-critical. Vision is degraded by dehydration, fatigue, glare, and aging. To see manned traffic and judge the aircraft's attitude and altitude:

  • Use sunglasses against glare during the day, but remove them in low light.
  • Scan the sky in a deliberate, systematic pattern rather than staring at one spot — the eye detects motion better with active scanning.
  • At night the eyes need about 30 minutes to adapt to darkness, and night vision relies on the off-center part of the eye. A bright light (a phone screen, headlights, a flashlight in the face) can destroy dark adaptation in an instant.

content.text: DRUGS & ALCOHOL (14 CFR 107.27 / 107.57): A remote pilot may NOT operate a small UAS within 8 hours of consuming alcohol ('8 hours bottle-to-throttle'), while under the influence of alcohol, with a blood-alcohol concentration of 0.04% or greater, or while using any drug that affects the person's faculties in a way contrary to safety.

content.html:
Alcohol, drugs, and medication

Alcohol impairs judgment, coordination, and reaction time, and its effects linger well after the legal 8-hour window — a hangover still impairs you even when your BAC has returned to zero. Many over-the-counter and prescription medications (antihistamines, sleep aids, some cold remedies) also impair faculties; if a drug affects your safety-related performance, you must not fly. When in doubt, ground yourself.

rows: Am I sick? Any symptom that could impair me?
Have I taken any drug (prescription or OTC) that could affect performance?
Am I under psychological pressure — work, family, deadlines?
Have I been drinking within 8 hours? Within 24? Is my BAC under 0.04%?
Am I tired or poorly rested?
Am I emotionally upset in a way that could distract me?

content.text: IMSAFE is a personal pre-flight checklist for the PILOT. Run it before every operation; if any item is a 'yes,' reconsider whether you should fly.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: You must be in a physical and mental condition to operate safely; dehydration, stress, and fatigue silently degrade judgment and vision.
Hyperventilation (over-breathing from stress) causes lightheadedness and is corrected by slowing the breathing rate.
Alcohol/drug rule: no operating within 8 hours of alcohol, while impaired, with BAC ≥ 0.04%, or while using any drug contrary to safety.
Night vision needs ~30 minutes to adapt and uses off-center viewing; bright light destroys it.
Use IMSAFE (Illness, Medication, Stress, Alcohol, Fatigue, Emotion) to self-assess before every flight.

flashcards.definition: A remote pilot may not operate within 8 hours of consuming alcohol, while under the influence, or with a blood-alcohol concentration of 0.04% or greater.
A pilot self-assessment: Illness, Medication, Stress, Alcohol, Fatigue, Emotion.
flashcards.hint_1: It's about the PILOT, not the aircraft.

flashcards.definition: Abnormally rapid/deep breathing (often from stress) that expels too much CO2, causing dizziness, tingling, and visual impairment; corrected by slowing the breathing rate.
Night vision takes about 30 minutes to fully develop and relies on off-center (peripheral) viewing; bright light destroys it quickly.
Acute or chronic tiredness that degrades attention, reaction time, and judgment — a leading human-factor hazard. Don't fly tired.

images_needed.caption: IMSAFE checklist graphic listing Illness, Medication, Stress, Alcohol, Fatigue, Emotion with self-check prompts.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Aeronautical Decision-Making & the Five Hazardous Attitudes
learning_objectives: Define Aeronautical Decision-Making (ADM) and describe the DECIDE model.
Name the five hazardous attitudes and state the correct antidote for each verbatim.
Recognize a hazardous attitude in a scenario and apply its antidote.

content.html:
What is ADM?

Aeronautical Decision-Making (ADM) is a systematic approach to consistently determining the best course of action in response to a given set of circumstances. It is what turns raw skill into safe flying. ADM teaches pilots to recognize hazards, evaluate risk, manage their own attitudes, and make conscious go/no-go decisions instead of reacting on instinct or emotion.

A common ADM process is the DECIDE model, a continuous loop:

  • Detect that a change has occurred.
  • Estimate the need to counter or react to the change.
  • Choose a desirable outcome.
  • Identify actions to control the change.
  • Do — take the necessary action.
  • Evaluate the effect of the action, then start the loop again.

A big part of ADM is honest self-assessment — recognizing the attitudes and pressures that push you toward an unsafe decision.

content.text: The five hazardous attitudes are some of the easiest points on the exam — memorize each attitude AND its exact antidote. Expect scenario questions that describe a pilot's thought and ask you to name the attitude or its antidote.

content.html:
The five hazardous attitudes

The FAA identifies five hazardous attitudes that interfere with sound decision-making. Each has a memorized antidote — a short statement a pilot consciously recalls to counter the attitude. Learn them word-for-word.

rows: The rules don't apply to me; I resent being told what to do.
Follow the rules. They are usually right.
I must act now, on my first idea, without thinking it through.
Accidents happen to other people, not to me.
I'll prove I'm better; I'll take the risk to show my skill.
I'm not really in control; what happens is up to luck or others.
I'm not helpless. I can make a difference.

sections.title: Example: spotting the attitude
sections.content:
A pilot needs an airspace authorization but launches anyway, thinking 'the FAA's rules are overkill for a quick job like this.' That is Anti-Authority; the antidote is 'Follow the rules. They are usually right.'

A pilot sees clouds building and a client waiting, and rushes the launch without finishing the preflight, thinking 'just go, sort it out in the air.' That is Impulsivity; the antidote is 'Not so fast. Think first.'

A pilot flies in marginal wind because 'I've flown in worse — I've got this.' That is Macho; the antidote is 'Taking chances is foolish.'

content.text: Don't confuse the attitudes: 'I can do it / I'll show them' is MACHO; 'the rules don't apply to me' is ANTI-AUTHORITY; 'it won't happen to me' is INVULNERABILITY. The exam tests these distinctions.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: ADM is a systematic approach to consistently choosing the best course of action; the DECIDE model is a continuous loop (Detect, Estimate, Choose, Identify, Do, Evaluate).
The five hazardous attitudes are Anti-Authority, Impulsivity, Invulnerability, Macho, and Resignation.
Each attitude has a memorized antidote — know them verbatim (e.g., Macho → 'Taking chances is foolish').
Recognizing the attitude in yourself is the first step to applying its antidote and making a safe decision.

flashcards.definition: A systematic approach to consistently determining the best course of action in response to a given set of circumstances.
Attitude: 'Don't tell me!' (the rules don't apply to me). Antidote: 'Follow the rules. They are usually right.'
Attitude: 'Do something quickly!' (act on the first thought). Antidote: 'Not so fast. Think first.'
Attitude: 'It won't happen to me.' Antidote: 'It could happen to me.'
Attitude: 'I can do it' (prove I'm better, take risks). Antidote: 'Taking chances is foolish.'
Attitude: 'What's the use?' (I'm not in control). Antidote: 'I'm not helpless. I can make a difference.'
A decision loop: Detect, Estimate, Choose, Identify, Do, Evaluate.

images_needed.caption: Table or infographic of the five hazardous attitudes paired with their antidotes (Anti-Authority, Impulsivity, Invulnerability, Macho, Resignation).

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Risk Management — PAVE & the 3P Model
learning_objectives: Apply the PAVE checklist to identify risk in the four categories: Pilot, Aircraft, enVironment, External pressures.
Use the 3P model (Perceive, Process, Perform) to manage identified risk.
Recognize how external pressure ('get-there-itis') drives unsafe decisions.

content.html:
From decision-making to risk management

ADM tells you how to decide; risk management gives you structured tools to find and reduce the hazards before they bite. The FAA promotes two simple frameworks every remote pilot should run before and during a flight: PAVE (to identify risk) and the 3P model (to act on it).

PAVE — find the risk in four buckets

The PAVE checklist breaks risk into four categories so nothing is overlooked:

  • P — Pilot: Am I fit to fly? (Run IMSAFE — illness, medication, stress, alcohol, fatigue, emotion. Am I current and proficient?)
  • A — Aircraft: Is the drone airworthy and adequate for this mission? (Battery state, props, firmware, payload, lighting for night.)
  • V — enVironment: Weather, airspace, terrain, obstacles, time of day, people and structures near the site.
  • E — External pressures: Schedule, the client, money, 'I drove two hours to get here' — anything pushing you to fly when you otherwise wouldn't.

The most dangerous of these is often External pressure, because it makes a pilot rationalize away the other three. The classic trap is 'get-there-itis' — pressing on with a flight to satisfy a deadline despite mounting hazards.

content.text: External pressure is a leading cause of bad go/no-go decisions. Build in an 'out' before you launch — decide in advance what conditions will make you cancel — so a client's deadline can't override your safety judgment.

rows: Gusty wind near a crowded park; battery only at 60%.
Evaluate the level of risk each hazard poses.
Wind plus crowd = high risk of injury if control is lost.
Take action to mitigate or eliminate the risk.
Move the operation away from people, wait for calmer wind, or cancel.

content.text: Remember the chain: PAVE finds the risks → 3P (Perceive, Process, Perform) is the loop you run to manage them → DECIDE is your moment-to-moment decision loop in flight. IMSAFE is the 'Pilot' part of PAVE.

content.alt: PAVE risk-management checklist showing Pilot, Aircraft, enVironment, External pressures
content.caption: Figure: The PAVE checklist — four categories of risk to assess before every operation.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: PAVE identifies risk in four buckets: Pilot, Aircraft, enVironment, External pressures.
The 'Pilot' element of PAVE is assessed with the IMSAFE checklist.
External pressure ('get-there-itis') is a leading cause of unsafe decisions — set personal minimums and an 'out' in advance.
The 3P model — Perceive, Process, Perform — is the loop used to manage identified risk.

flashcards.definition: A risk-identification tool with four categories: Pilot, Aircraft, enVironment, and External pressures.
A risk-management process: Perceive the hazards, Process the level of risk, Perform a mitigating action.
Outside influences — schedule, client, money, sunk cost — that push a pilot to fly when conditions say otherwise; the source of 'get-there-itis.'
The unsafe tendency to continue a flight to meet a deadline or goal despite increasing hazards; a product of external pressure.

images_needed.caption: PAVE checklist infographic: Pilot, Aircraft, enVironment, External pressures, each with example risk items.

lessons.title: Lesson 5: Radio Communications, Airport Operations & Traffic Patterns
learning_objectives: Identify CTAF/UNICOM and use correct self-announce phraseology at a non-towered airport.
Name the legs of the standard traffic pattern and the standard direction of turns.
Determine the runway in use and avoid manned traffic on departure and final approach.

content.html:
Why a drone pilot cares about airports

Most sUAS sites are near non-towered (uncontrolled) airports, where there is no control tower sequencing traffic. Manned aircraft self-announce their intentions on a shared radio frequency, and a remote pilot who monitors that frequency gains powerful situational awareness — you can hear an aircraft announce it is on final approach or departing and keep your drone well clear. Remember: under 107.37 you must yield the right of way to all aircraft and never operate so close as to create a collision hazard.

CTAF and UNICOM

The CTAF (Common Traffic Advisory Frequency) is the frequency pilots use to self-announce position and intentions at a non-towered airport. It is often the same as the UNICOM (a non-government air/ground radio commonly 122.7, 122.8, 122.9, or 123.0). On a sectional chart, the CTAF is shown with a 'C' in a magenta box near the airport data. A remote pilot is not required to transmit, but monitoring the CTAF to track manned traffic is excellent practice. Self-announce phraseology follows a consistent format: '[Airport] traffic, [aircraft], [position/intentions], [Airport].' — for example, 'Smithville traffic, Cessna Three-Four-X, on left base runway one-eight, Smithville.'

content.text: To fly in CONTROLLED airspace around a towered airport (Class B, C, D, or surface-area E) you need an airspace authorization (LAANC/DroneZone) in advance — monitoring the radio does not substitute for authorization.

content.html:
The standard traffic pattern

Manned aircraft fly a rectangular traffic pattern around a runway. Knowing its shape tells you where to expect aircraft so you can keep your drone away from them. The legs, in order, are:

  • Upwind — climbing out aligned with the runway after takeoff.
  • Crosswind — a 90° turn off the upwind leg.
  • Downwind — flown parallel to the runway, opposite the landing direction (usually at pattern altitude, ~1,000 ft AGL for light aircraft).
  • Base — a 90° turn from downwind toward the runway.
  • Final — aligned with the runway, descending to land.

Standard pattern turns are to the left unless the chart or airport markings specify right traffic. The greatest collision risk to a drone is from aircraft departing or on final approach, because that is where manned aircraft are low — in the altitude band where sUAS operate.

content.alt: Standard left-hand traffic pattern showing upwind, crosswind, downwind, base, and final legs around a runway
content.caption: Figure: Standard left-hand traffic pattern — upwind, crosswind, downwind, base, final. Drones must avoid aircraft on departure and final, where manned traffic is low.

content.html:
Determining the runway in use

Aircraft take off and land into the wind, so the wind tells you which runway is active. At a non-towered field, wind direction is shown by a wind sock, a tetrahedron, or a segmented circle near the runway. If the wind sock points from the south, aircraft are likely using the south-facing runway — and you should expect departures and approaches along that line and stay clear.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: CTAF is the self-announce frequency at non-towered airports (often the same as UNICOM); monitoring it builds traffic awareness.
Under 107.37 the remote pilot must yield the right of way to all aircraft and avoid creating a collision hazard.
Traffic-pattern legs in order: upwind, crosswind, downwind, base, final; standard turns are LEFT unless otherwise charted.
Aircraft take off and land into the wind; use a wind sock/tetrahedron/segmented circle to find the runway in use.
Watch especially for aircraft on departure and final approach — that's where manned traffic flies low, near drone altitudes.

flashcards.definition: The radio frequency used by pilots to self-announce position and intentions at a non-towered airport; often the same as the UNICOM frequency.
flashcards.hint_1: Shown as a 'C' in a magenta box on a sectional.

flashcards.definition: A non-government air/ground communication station at some airports (commonly 122.7/122.8/122.9/123.0); frequently serves as the CTAF.
Upwind, crosswind, downwind, base, and final — the rectangular path manned aircraft fly around a runway; standard turns are to the left.
flashcards.hint_1: Five legs, left turns by default.

flashcards.definition: Determined by wind direction (aircraft take off and land into the wind), indicated by a wind sock, tetrahedron, or segmented circle.
A remote pilot must yield the right of way to all other aircraft and may not operate so close as to create a collision hazard.

images_needed.caption: Standard left-hand traffic pattern diagram labeling upwind, crosswind, downwind, base, and final around a runway, with a wind sock indicating wind direction.
Airport wind indicators: wind sock, tetrahedron, and segmented circle, showing how each indicates wind/landing direction.

lessons.title: Lesson 6: Maintenance, Preflight Inspection & Recordkeeping
learning_objectives: Describe a proper sUAS maintenance program and what records to keep.
Perform a preflight inspection per 14 CFR 107.15 and 107.49.
Identify items to check before flight: aircraft condition, weather, airspace, power, and Remote ID.

content.html:
Maintenance and condition for safe operation

Under 14 CFR 107.15, no person may operate a small UAS unless it is in a condition for safe operation, and the RPIC must discontinue the flight if it is no longer safe. Maintenance means keeping the aircraft and its components in working order. Follow the manufacturer's maintenance instructions if provided; if none exist, establish your own scheduled-maintenance program. Inspect and service the items that wear or fail:

  • Propellers — check for cracks, nicks, chips, and warping; replace damaged props.
  • Motors — listen and feel for roughness, grinding, or vibration.
  • Airframe — check arms, frame, and mounts for cracks or loose hardware.
  • Battery — inspect for swelling/puffing, damage, and correct charge; retire damaged packs.
  • Control station, firmware, and software — keep firmware current and verify the link works.

content.text: Keep maintenance and inspection RECORDS: what was done, when, and by whom. Good recordkeeping shows the aircraft was maintained and helps you spot recurring problems before they cause a failure.

content.html:
Preflight: 107.49 familiarization, inspection, and actions

Before each flight, 14 CFR 107.49 requires the RPIC to:

  • Assess the operating environment — local weather, airspace and any flight restrictions, the location of persons and property on the surface, and other ground hazards.
  • Confirm that all persons directly participating understand the operation, weather, and emergency procedures (brief the crew).
  • Ensure all control links between the control station and the aircraft are working.
  • Verify there is enough power to operate for the intended time (sufficient battery, with reserve).
  • Confirm any object attached/carried is secure and won't adversely affect flight.
  • Verify that Remote ID equipment is functioning (when required).

The preflight inspection (107.15) confirms the airframe, props, battery, and control station are in condition for safe operation. If anything fails the check, don't fly until it's corrected.

rows: Props, motors, airframe, battery (no swelling), secured payload, firmware current.
Weather (wind, visibility, clouds), airspace class & authorizations, NOTAMs/TFRs, people/structures nearby.
Sufficient battery with reserve; control link verified; lost-link/RTH settings confirmed; Remote ID functioning.
Crew briefed on roles and emergencies; proof of registration available; certificate/recency in order.

content.text: Use a written checklist for preflight. A swollen/puffed battery, a nicked prop, or a failed control link must ground the aircraft — these are not 'fly and see' items.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Under 107.15 the sUAS must be in a condition for safe operation, and the RPIC must stop the flight if it becomes unsafe.
Follow the manufacturer's maintenance schedule (or your own); inspect props, motors, airframe, battery, and control station; keep records.
Preflight under 107.49 covers environment/weather/airspace, crew briefing, control links, sufficient power, secured payload, and Remote ID.
Damaged props, swollen batteries, and failed links are no-go items — correct them before flight.

flashcards.definition: No one may operate an sUAS unless it is in a condition for safe operation; the RPIC must discontinue the flight if it becomes unsafe.
Before flight the RPIC must assess weather/airspace/ground hazards, brief crew, verify control links, ensure sufficient power, secure payloads, and confirm Remote ID is working.
Check props for cracks, nicks, chips, and warping before each flight; replace any damaged propeller.
A LiPo pack that has swelled is damaged and a fire risk — retire it immediately; never charge or fly a swollen battery.

images_needed.caption: Preflight inspection checklist graphic grouping checks into aircraft, environment, power/links, and crew/documents.

lessons.title: Lesson 7: Emergency Procedures, Night Operations & Maintaining VLOS
learning_objectives: Respond correctly to lost link/flyaway, GPS loss, low battery, and fire emergencies.
Apply 107.9 reporting requirements and identify fire-extinguisher classes and basic first aid.
Conduct safe night operations, recognize visual illusions, and maintain visual line of sight.

content.html:
Planning for the emergency before it happens

The remote PIC's job in an emergency is to protect people and property on the ground. The single most important habit is to know your aircraft's failsafe behavior before you launch and to position your operation so that, if control is lost, the aircraft comes down over the least-populated area. Common emergencies and the correct response:

  • Lost link / flyaway: If the radio link is lost, most aircraft execute a programmed failsafe — typically return-to-home (RTH), hover, or land. Know which yours does and verify the RTH point and altitude before flight. If a true flyaway occurs, attempt to regain control and be ready to report it.
  • Loss of GPS: The aircraft may drift or switch to a manual/attitude mode. Be prepared to fly it manually, keep it in sight, and land promptly in a clear area.
  • Low battery: Land immediately at the nearest safe spot — don't try to finish the mission. Always plan a battery reserve and never fly to 0%.
  • In-flight emergency: 14 CFR 107.21 permits the RPIC to deviate from any rule of Part 107 to the extent necessary to meet an in-flight emergency. If you do, you may be asked to send a written report to the FAA upon request.

content.text: Accident reporting (14 CFR 107.9): Report to the FAA within 10 calendar days if an operation results in serious injury or loss of consciousness, OR property damage (other than to the drone) where repair cost OR fair market value exceeds $500.

content.html:
Fire, first aid, and extinguisher classes

LiPo batteries can enter thermal runaway — an intense, self-sustaining fire — if punctured, overcharged, over-discharged, or damaged. Keep a plan and a way to fight a small fire, and store packs in a fireproof container. Know the basic fire-extinguisher classes so you grab the right one:

  • Class A — ordinary combustibles (wood, paper, cloth).
  • Class B — flammable liquids (fuel, oil).
  • Class C — energized electrical equipment.
  • Class D — combustible metals (including some lithium metal fires).

Because drone fires involve electronics and lithium chemistry, an extinguisher rated for Class C (electrical) — and ideally Class D for metal fires — plus copious water/cooling to prevent reignition is the practical approach. Have basic first-aid supplies and knowledge on site; in any injury, care for people first, then preserve the scene and meet 107.9 reporting duties.

rows: Let the programmed failsafe (RTH/hover/land) run; know it in advance; attempt to regain control.
Try to regain control; track the aircraft; be ready to report; ensure it endangers no one.
Switch to manual flight, keep VLOS, land promptly in a clear area.
Land immediately at the nearest safe location; never push past reserve.
Use a Class C/D-appropriate extinguisher; cool to prevent reignition; keep people clear.

content.html:
Night operations and visual illusions

Since the 2021 rule, night flight is allowed without a waiver if the aircraft has anti-collision lighting visible for at least 3 statute miles with a flash rate sufficient to avoid a collision (107.29), and the remote pilot has completed the required training. At night, plan for degraded human vision: night vision takes ~30 minutes to adapt, depth perception and the ability to judge the drone's distance and attitude are reduced, and a steady light far away can be hard to distinguish from a moving one. Use off-center viewing and active scanning, survey the area in daylight beforehand, and verify all lighting works before launch. Watch for illusions — a single light against a dark background can appear to move (autokinesis), and isolated lights make distance hard to judge.

content.text: Maintain Visual Line of Sight (107.31): The remote PIC, person manipulating the controls, and any VO must be able to see the unmanned aircraft with vision UNAIDED (corrective lenses allowed) throughout the flight. First-person-view (FPV) goggles do NOT satisfy VLOS — use a VO if you fly FPV. Keep the aircraft close enough to know its location, attitude, altitude, and direction, and to see and avoid traffic.

content.html:
Visual scanning, VLOS, and operating near people

Continuously and systematically scan the airspace for other aircraft — yield to all of them (107.37). Use a visual observer to extend your coverage on large or busy sites. Keep the aircraft within unaided VLOS at all times; if you momentarily lose sight, regain it immediately or land. When operating near people or structures, position yourself so the aircraft is never directly over non-participants unless you meet an Operations Over People category, keep a safe buffer, and plan your emergency landing zones away from crowds. Security matters too: be alert to suspicious activity near sensitive sites, respect restricted areas and TFRs, and protect any sensitive imagery you collect.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Know your aircraft's lost-link failsafe (RTH/hover/land) before launch; on low battery, land immediately; 107.21 lets you deviate from rules to meet an in-flight emergency.
Report to the FAA within 10 days (107.9) for serious injury / loss of consciousness or property damage over $500 (excluding the drone).
Fire classes: A combustibles, B liquids, C electrical, D metals; treat LiPo fires as electrical/metal and cool to prevent reignition.
Night flight needs anti-collision lighting visible 3 SM (107.29); allow ~30 min for dark adaptation, use off-center viewing, and watch for illusions like autokinesis.
Maintain unaided VLOS at all times (107.31) — FPV alone does not satisfy it; scan continuously, yield to all aircraft, and keep emergency landing zones away from people.

flashcards.definition: When the control link is lost, the aircraft runs its programmed failsafe — typically return-to-home, hover, or land. Know and verify this behavior before every flight.
The remote PIC may deviate from any rule of Part 107 to the extent necessary to meet an in-flight emergency; a written report may be required upon FAA request.
Report to the FAA within 10 calendar days for serious injury/loss of consciousness, or property damage (other than the drone) exceeding $500 in repair cost or fair market value.
A = ordinary combustibles; B = flammable liquids; C = energized electrical; D = combustible metals. Drone/LiPo fires are treated as electrical (C) and/or metal (D).
For civil twilight and night operations, the aircraft must have anti-collision lighting visible for at least 3 statute miles with an adequate flash rate.
The RPIC, person manipulating controls, and any VO must keep the aircraft in sight with vision unaided (corrective lenses OK) throughout the flight; FPV alone does not satisfy VLOS.

images_needed.caption: Decision flowchart for in-flight emergencies: lost link, flyaway, GPS loss, low battery, fire — with the correct response for each.
Night drone operation showing anti-collision lighting visible 3 SM and a remote pilot using off-center scanning.

quiz_questions.question_text: Who is directly responsible for, and the final authority over, a small UAS operation conducted under Part 107?
options: The remote pilot in command (RPIC)
The person manipulating the flight controls
The visual observer
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The remote pilot in command (RPIC)
quiz_questions.explanation: Under 14 CFR 107.19, the RPIC is directly responsible for, and is the final authority over, the operation. A non-certificated person may manipulate the controls only under the RPIC's direct supervision, and a VO only assists.

quiz_questions.question_text: Crew Resource Management (CRM) is best described as:
options: The effective use of all available resources — people, hardware, and information — to operate safely
A requirement that every operation use at least two certificated remote pilots
The manufacturer's recommended maintenance schedule for the aircraft
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The effective use of all available resources — people, hardware, and information — to operate safely
quiz_questions.explanation: CRM means making effective use of all resources available to conduct the flight safely. It does not require multiple pilots, and it is unrelated to a maintenance schedule.

quiz_questions.question_text: A person who does not hold a remote pilot certificate wishes to fly the drone. Under Part 107, this is permitted only if:
options: A certificated remote PIC directly supervises and can immediately take direct control of the aircraft
The person has passed the TRUST recreational test
The flight stays below 100 feet AGL
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A certificated remote PIC directly supervises and can immediately take direct control of the aircraft
quiz_questions.explanation: A non-certificated person may manipulate the controls only when an RPIC supervises and can immediately take direct control. TRUST is for recreational flying, and altitude does not change the certification requirement.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under Part 107, a remote pilot may NOT operate a small UAS:
options: Within 8 hours of consuming alcohol, while impaired, or with a blood-alcohol concentration of 0.04% or greater
Within 4 hours of consuming alcohol, or with a BAC of 0.08% or greater
Within 12 hours of consuming alcohol, or with a BAC of 0.02% or greater
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Within 8 hours of consuming alcohol, while impaired, or with a blood-alcohol concentration of 0.04% or greater
quiz_questions.explanation: 14 CFR 107.27/107.57 set the limits at 8 hours bottle-to-throttle, no operating while under the influence, and a BAC of 0.04% or greater. The other options use incorrect numbers.

quiz_questions.question_text: A remote pilot becomes anxious before a difficult job and begins breathing rapidly, then feels lightheaded and tingling in the fingers. The most likely cause and corrective action is:
options: Hyperventilation; consciously slow the breathing rate until normal breathing returns
Hypoxia; descend the aircraft immediately
Dehydration; the pilot should stop scanning for traffic
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Hyperventilation; consciously slow the breathing rate until normal breathing returns
quiz_questions.explanation: Rapid breathing from stress expels too much carbon dioxide, causing lightheadedness and tingling — hyperventilation. The fix is to slow the breathing rate. Hypoxia (oxygen deficiency from altitude) and dehydration are different conditions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which checklist is used by a pilot to assess his or her OWN fitness for flight?
options: IMSAFE (Illness, Medication, Stress, Alcohol, Fatigue, Emotion)
PAVE (Pilot, Aircraft, enVironment, External pressures)
DECIDE (Detect, Estimate, Choose, Identify, Do, Evaluate)
quiz_questions.correct_answer: IMSAFE (Illness, Medication, Stress, Alcohol, Fatigue, Emotion)
quiz_questions.explanation: IMSAFE is the personal-fitness self-check. PAVE is a broader risk-identification tool (the pilot is only one element), and DECIDE is a decision-making loop.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pilot took an over-the-counter antihistamine for allergies an hour before a planned operation. What is the correct action?
options: Do not fly if the medication could impair faculties in a way contrary to safety
Fly normally, because only prescription drugs are restricted under Part 107
Fly, but limit the operation to under 200 feet AGL
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Do not fly if the medication could impair faculties in a way contrary to safety
quiz_questions.explanation: Part 107 prohibits operating while using ANY drug — over-the-counter or prescription — that affects faculties contrary to safety. Many antihistamines cause drowsiness, so the pilot should not fly. Altitude does not mitigate impairment.

quiz_questions.question_text: Aeronautical Decision-Making (ADM) is best defined as:
options: A systematic approach to consistently determining the best course of action for a given set of circumstances
A federal requirement to file a flight plan before every operation
The manufacturer's procedure for calibrating the aircraft's compass
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A systematic approach to consistently determining the best course of action for a given set of circumstances
quiz_questions.explanation: ADM is the systematic process pilots use to make sound decisions. It is not a flight-plan requirement or a calibration procedure.

quiz_questions.question_text: A remote pilot decides to fly in airspace that requires an authorization without obtaining one, reasoning that 'those rules are excessive for a quick job like this.' Which hazardous attitude is this, and what is its antidote?
options: Anti-Authority; 'Follow the rules. They are usually right.'
Macho; 'Taking chances is foolish.'
Resignation; 'I'm not helpless. I can make a difference.'
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Anti-Authority; 'Follow the rules. They are usually right.'
quiz_questions.explanation: Disregarding rules because they seem unnecessary is the Anti-Authority ('Don't tell me!') attitude; the antidote is 'Follow the rules. They are usually right.' Macho is about proving skill; Resignation is feeling not in control.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is the correct antidote for the MACHO hazardous attitude?
options: Taking chances is foolish.
It could happen to me.
Not so fast. Think first.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Taking chances is foolish.
quiz_questions.explanation: Macho ('I can do it,' taking unnecessary risks to prove skill) is countered by 'Taking chances is foolish.' 'It could happen to me' is the Invulnerability antidote; 'Not so fast. Think first.' is the Impulsivity antidote.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pilot feels rushed by a waiting client and launches before completing the preflight, acting on the first idea that comes to mind. Which hazardous attitude is displayed, and what is its antidote?
options: Impulsivity; 'Not so fast. Think first.'
Resignation; 'I'm not helpless. I can make a difference.'
Invulnerability; 'It could happen to me.'
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Impulsivity; 'Not so fast. Think first.'
quiz_questions.explanation: Acting immediately on the first thought without thinking it through is Impulsivity; the antidote is 'Not so fast. Think first.'

quiz_questions.question_text: When a series of problems overwhelms a pilot and they think 'What's the use? Whatever happens, happens,' the correct antidote is:
options: I'm not helpless. I can make a difference.
Follow the rules. They are usually right.
Taking chances is foolish.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: I'm not helpless. I can make a difference.
quiz_questions.explanation: That thought is the Resignation attitude; its antidote is 'I'm not helpless. I can make a difference.' The others are antidotes for Anti-Authority and Macho.

quiz_questions.question_text: The PAVE checklist divides risk into four categories. What do the letters represent?
options: Pilot, Aircraft, enVironment, External pressures
Power, Altitude, Visibility, Endurance
Pilot, Airspace, Visibility, Equipment
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Pilot, Aircraft, enVironment, External pressures
quiz_questions.explanation: PAVE = Pilot, Aircraft, enVironment, and External pressures — a structured way to identify risk before and during flight.

quiz_questions.question_text: A remote pilot drove two hours to a job site and the client is pressing to finish today, even though winds are picking up beyond the pilot's comfort level. Which element of PAVE is most directly creating risk, and what is the danger?
options: External pressures; it can lead to 'get-there-itis' and an unsafe go decision
Aircraft; the drone is not airworthy
Pilot; the pilot is intoxicated
quiz_questions.correct_answer: External pressures; it can lead to 'get-there-itis' and an unsafe go decision
quiz_questions.explanation: Schedule, sunk cost, and client pressure are External pressures, the classic driver of 'get-there-itis.' Nothing indicates the aircraft is unairworthy or the pilot is intoxicated.

quiz_questions.question_text: The 3P risk-management model consists of which three steps?
options: Perceive, Process, Perform
Plan, Prepare, Proceed
Predict, Prevent, Protect
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Perceive, Process, Perform
quiz_questions.explanation: The 3P model is Perceive the hazards, Process the level of risk, and Perform a risk-mitigating action.

quiz_questions.question_text: While operating near a non-towered airport, the best way for a remote pilot to maintain awareness of nearby manned traffic is to:
options: Monitor the CTAF (Common Traffic Advisory Frequency)
Call the nearest control tower for a clearance
File a flight plan with Flight Service
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Monitor the CTAF (Common Traffic Advisory Frequency)
quiz_questions.explanation: At a non-towered airport, pilots self-announce on the CTAF. Monitoring it lets the remote pilot anticipate manned traffic. There is no tower to call, and Part 107 does not require a flight plan.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are the legs of a standard airport traffic pattern, in order?
options: Upwind, crosswind, downwind, base, final
Departure, transition, arrival, approach, landing
Final, base, downwind, crosswind, upwind, repeated clockwise
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Upwind, crosswind, downwind, base, final
quiz_questions.explanation: The standard pattern legs in sequence are upwind, crosswind, downwind, base, and final, with left turns unless otherwise charted.

quiz_questions.question_text: At a non-towered airport, how does a remote pilot determine which runway manned aircraft are most likely using?
options: By the wind direction, shown by a wind sock, tetrahedron, or segmented circle (aircraft take off and land into the wind)
By the lowest-numbered runway, which is always active
By the magenta box on the sectional chart
quiz_questions.correct_answer: By the wind direction, shown by a wind sock, tetrahedron, or segmented circle (aircraft take off and land into the wind)
quiz_questions.explanation: Aircraft take off and land into the wind, so wind indicators reveal the runway in use. Runways are not chosen by number, and the magenta box denotes the CTAF, not the active runway.

quiz_questions.question_text: When operating a drone near an airport, manned aircraft pose the greatest collision risk when they are:
options: On departure or final approach, where they fly low — near typical drone altitudes
At cruising altitude well above 400 feet AGL
Parked on the ramp
quiz_questions.correct_answer: On departure or final approach, where they fly low — near typical drone altitudes
quiz_questions.explanation: Aircraft are lowest during departure and final approach, putting them in the same altitude band as sUAS. The remote pilot must yield and stay clear there.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under 14 CFR 107.15, what must the remote PIC do if, during flight, the aircraft is no longer in a condition for safe operation?
options: Discontinue the flight
Continue to the planned landing point regardless
Climb to 400 feet AGL and hover
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Discontinue the flight
quiz_questions.explanation: 107.15 requires the sUAS to be in a condition for safe operation, and the RPIC must discontinue the flight if it is no longer safe.

quiz_questions.question_text: During a preflight inspection, a remote pilot notices the LiPo battery is swollen (puffed). The correct action is to:
options: Retire the battery immediately and not use it; do not charge or fly it
Fly a short mission to drain it, then dispose of it
Charge it fully first to reduce the swelling
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Retire the battery immediately and not use it; do not charge or fly it
quiz_questions.explanation: A swollen LiPo is damaged and a fire risk. It must be retired immediately — never charged or flown. Draining or charging it increases the danger.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is required as part of the preflight familiarization, inspection, and actions under 14 CFR 107.49?
options: Assess the operating environment, ensure control links work, verify sufficient power, and confirm Remote ID is functioning
File a notice with the nearest air traffic control tower
Obtain a third-class medical certificate
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Assess the operating environment, ensure control links work, verify sufficient power, and confirm Remote ID is functioning
quiz_questions.explanation: 107.49 requires assessing weather/airspace/ground hazards, briefing crew, verifying control links and sufficient power, securing payloads, and confirming Remote ID. No tower notice or medical certificate is required under Part 107.

quiz_questions.question_text: A remote pilot loses the control link to the aircraft mid-flight. What should the pilot have done BEFORE launch to prepare for this?
options: Know and verify the aircraft's lost-link failsafe behavior (e.g., return-to-home altitude and point)
Disable the return-to-home feature to allow manual recovery
Plan to fly the drone to 0% battery to maximize coverage
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Know and verify the aircraft's lost-link failsafe behavior (e.g., return-to-home altitude and point)
quiz_questions.explanation: Knowing the failsafe (RTH/hover/land) and verifying the RTH point and altitude before flight is essential. Disabling RTH and flying to 0% battery both increase risk.

quiz_questions.question_text: An operation results in a bystander being knocked unconscious. Under 14 CFR 107.9, the remote PIC must report this to the FAA within:
options: 10 calendar days
24 hours
30 calendar days
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 10 calendar days
quiz_questions.explanation: 107.9 requires reporting within 10 calendar days for any serious injury or loss of consciousness, or for property damage (other than to the drone) exceeding $500.

quiz_questions.question_text: A drone operation causes property damage to a parked car. The repair will cost $350 and the car's fair market value far exceeds that. Must this be reported to the FAA under 107.9?
options: No — the repair cost does not exceed $500 and no serious injury occurred
Yes — all property damage must always be reported
Yes — because a vehicle was involved
quiz_questions.correct_answer: No — the repair cost does not exceed $500 and no serious injury occurred
quiz_questions.explanation: Reporting is required only if there is serious injury/loss of consciousness or property damage over $500 (by repair cost or fair market value). A $350 repair with no injury does not trigger the report.

quiz_questions.question_text: Per 14 CFR 107.21, what may a remote PIC do when confronted with an in-flight emergency?
options: Deviate from any rule of Part 107 to the extent necessary to meet the emergency
Continue the operation exactly as planned, since no deviation is ever allowed
Hand control to a non-certificated bystander
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Deviate from any rule of Part 107 to the extent necessary to meet the emergency
quiz_questions.explanation: 107.21 allows the RPIC to deviate from any rule of Part 107 to the extent necessary to meet an in-flight emergency; a written report may be required upon FAA request.

quiz_questions.question_text: To operate at night under Part 107 without a waiver, the small unmanned aircraft must:
options: Have anti-collision lighting visible for at least 3 statute miles with a flash rate sufficient to avoid a collision
Be flown below 100 feet AGL at all times
Carry a landing light visible for 1 statute mile
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Have anti-collision lighting visible for at least 3 statute miles with a flash rate sufficient to avoid a collision
quiz_questions.explanation: Under 107.29, night and civil-twilight operations require anti-collision lighting visible for at least 3 statute miles with an adequate flash rate, plus completion of the required training.

quiz_questions.question_text: A remote pilot wants to fly using FPV (first-person-view) goggles. To remain compliant with the visual-line-of-sight rule (107.31), the pilot must:
options: Use a visual observer who maintains unaided sight of the aircraft at all times
Simply keep the aircraft within 1,000 feet
Do nothing extra — FPV goggles satisfy the VLOS requirement
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Use a visual observer who maintains unaided sight of the aircraft at all times
quiz_questions.explanation: VLOS requires the aircraft be seen with vision unaided (corrective lenses OK). FPV goggles do not satisfy VLOS, so a visual observer must keep the aircraft in sight.

quiz_questions.question_text: A small electrical fire starts in the drone's electronics after a hard landing. Which extinguisher class is appropriate for energized electrical equipment?
options: Class C
Class A
Class K
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Class C
quiz_questions.explanation: Class C extinguishers are rated for energized electrical fires. Class A is for ordinary combustibles; Class K is for cooking oils/fats. (Lithium-metal fires also involve Class D and require cooling to prevent reignition.)

quiz_questions.question_text: While maintaining VLOS at night, a remote pilot stares directly at the aircraft's single light and it appears to drift, even though the drone is holding a steady hover. This illusion is best avoided by:
options: Using off-center viewing and active scanning instead of staring at one fixed point
Increasing the brightness of the control-station screen
Removing corrective lenses to sharpen night vision
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Using off-center viewing and active scanning instead of staring at one fixed point
quiz_questions.explanation: Staring at a single light in the dark can produce autokinesis (apparent motion). Off-center viewing and active scanning preserve night vision and reduce the illusion. Bright screens destroy dark adaptation, and removing needed lenses degrades vision.
